INTRODUCTION.
The North American Republic consists of twenty-four regularly
organized States, which with the exception of some mountain ridges
is wholly susceptible of cultivation. It contains upwards of two
millions of square miles, is therefore thirteen times as large as
France; twenty-three times as large as England, and would, were
it peopled as densely as those countries, contain four hundred
millions of people. It is watered on its eastern, western, and on
part of its southern sides by the ocean, and on part of its northern
irontier by immense lakes. It is intersected in all directions by
magnificent rivers, and has more facilities for water conveyance
than any other continental country, it abounds in mines and minerals,
and produces every thing necessary for the sustenance and enjoy-
ment of mankind.
The population of the United States has been several times
doubled, in periods of less than twenty-five years, it now contains
upwards of twelve millions of people, and bids fair at no very
distant period, to be more populous and powerful than any nation
ever was.
Such is the present state, such the present prospects of a nation
originally settled by men who either fled from religious intolerance
and persecution to seek peace in the wilderness, or men whose
notions of liberty were too high to permit them to endure the tyranny
they were subjected to at home; or of men who sought their for-

ORIGINAL POETRY.
They that be slain with the sword are better than they that be
elain with hunger; for these pine away, stricken through for want of
the fruits of the field.-Lamentations, ch. iv. v. 9.
Ah! wherefore weep my mother land,
Since Pharbus smiles with kindly ray,
And summer strews with bounteous hand
The joyous earth with flow rets gay?
Some in pleasure's car at eve'a,
Thro' the park and o'er the lake,
By zephyr breath or œlus driven,
In satiate draughts of sorrow make.
Yet others gnaw the clankless chain
Of want, which binds a free-born race,
Who, fetter'd, moan to bite again
Those links Britannia's muse disgrace.
The sickle comes, and nature yields
Her harvest's fruits and vineyard's wine,
And till a sword each subject wields,
To servile death devoted pine.
Did Abas mock the Lord of Hosts
To monarch's sounding glory knell ;
The tombs give up our fatber's ghosts-
They echo, but how Pharoah fell,
Invoking vengeance from the skies:
The woe-struck spirits of the past
Call on the living to arise,
Or freedom dare, or thraldom blast.
Our orphan's curse, the bridal night
In wedless charm our virgins fall,
And nature mourns their passion's blight,
And spreads anoa cold beauty's pall.
The dolant songsters of the grove,
Thro' fields of air on music borne,
Reflective shone the fruits of love,
And twilight weds 'em 'nea

FRAGMENT ON THE ORIGIN OF KINGS,
BY SIR WILLIAM JONES.
When Time was young,
And earth was clad in Nature's rudest garb,
Dark tangled forests, desarts vast and drear,
Wild heaths, and reedy lakes, and rushy fens;
When fresh and vigʼrous from the Eternal hand,
Man trod the rough domain; himself as rough,
The business of his life to propagate,
To draw nutrition, and to keep at bay
Instinct's ferocious swarms; then the wide world
Was but a huge estate, Heaven the prime Lord,
And all mankind his equal tegantry.
No power was known, have that which Nature owns-
Paternal sway-clad in the spoils of brutes,
And unrestrained as in the mountain's blast,
Dauntless and firm the sturdy savage roam'd,
His family a state-himself a chief.
Water, wild fruits, and animal repasts,
Compos'd his worldly good; with these in view,
On the rough margin of some stream or lake,
Begirt with matted brakes and forests tall,
Hle roar'd, with unskill'd hand, his wattled ebed.
Around him, nimble as the bounding roe,
His naked offspring play'd. Time brought desires,
And from desires which to repress was sin,
Full many a progeny room frolick'd round-
Affection filial, fondness for the seat
Of all their youth

ne by one diminished,
Population's kept in check. (a)
Pheasants; feather'd aristocracy!
Stately stalk in rainbow plumes;
Eclipsing partridge, brown democracy,
Ah! ye must feed the kitchen fumes,
Hares the juicy herbage cropping,
Trembling quick in every breath;
Running now, and now they're stopping-
All around is big with death.
Red slaughter's now without a muzzle,
And leaps from out each screening bush,
To 'scape which, is the poor game's puzzle,
And their sweet life's not worth a rush.
About the right to take this life;
That's question which admits dispute,
All who for argument are rife
May here discuss 'tween man and brute. (b)
But argumentative I'm not,
For "
MIGHT IS RIGHT," 'tis shortly said;
The man has power, the game has not,
He points his gun and shoots it dead.
And argument availeth not,
And reason's wasted upon power;
To fear alone 'twill yield a jot,
Nor that until the eleventh hour,
But who are these in russet jackets,
Millers' hats, with fowling pieces,
Shot-belts, dogs, and powder-flaskets,
Buckskin leggings, and white breeches?
Squires, knights, and lords, these men are dubbed ;
Of orders, privileged beyond
Those of bare walls and empty cupboard,
To shoot the game and fish the pond,
Whence this proud privilege-its rise?
'Mongst men of the same common clay;.
From money sprung; it has its price,
Which price a poor man cannot pay.
Though pay he cannot, eat he can
If shoot he must not, poach he will-
With right to game as good as any man,
He's three months' swea

IMPROVEMENT IN PADDLE-W¡IEELS.-A small model of a
new invention in this way has been sent to our office for inspec-
tion, accompanied by a letter from the inventor, pointing out
the differences between it and the common paddle-wheel, and
its superiority over them in various points; the most mate-
rial of which is, that the paddles in the machine turn upon two
pivots placed at the end, and work in holes at the sides or arms
of the wheel; and, from being thus fixed, the paddles would
all remain perpendicular when the wheel is at rest; these over-
balance from the pivot being downwards, if not prevented by
stop-pers. When in motion the paddle is kept firmly against
the pers until it passes the bottom or perpendicular line, when
the overbalance, which, from the pivot, is upwards, will gently
turn it over, and it will rise perpendicular, with its thin edge
out of the water, leaving the latter without any back wave or
swell: it is, therefore, formed on the principle of not lifting the
hack wave.
Two patents have lately been taken out for paddle-
wheels to prevent the sea-lift; and trials are about to be made
of them upon the largest scale; and this, certainly, is the only
true method of ascertaining their capabilities. The maker of
this machine signs himself James Carol, 38, Tufton-street, West-
minster, where he will show the machine each day from ten
until two o'clock.-Herald.

RUSSIA. The power of Russia extends itself over two
worlds; her progress is immense, and nothing seems able to
restrain it. While on the European side she has gained pos-
session of Poland, of Finland, of Tartary, of Bessarabia, and of
half Moldavia, on the Asiatic frontier her dominion is extended
to the Caspian Sea and the Caucasus, as far as the desert of
Kirgish. The Russian eagle hovers over the fertile plains of
Bulgaria, and beholds with pleasure the attractive soil of l'ersia
and the arid mountains of Armenia. There is nothing to dread
in the aggrandisement of Russia in Europe; the whole of the
western nations would rise en masse if she were to assume a
hostile attitude on the shores of the Baluc, of the Black Sea,
or on the banks of the Oder. The tottering empire of the
Turks would be protected by the Anglo-French alliance, which
Austria would join, although on this side the position of Silis-
tria would sufficiently protect Russia. In Asia, on the con-
trary, all the chances are in favour of Russia; perhaps even
while we write, her arms have penetrated to the south of the
Caucasus and Caspian Sea, and if this progress be not arrested

EXTRAORDINARY SEA-FISH POND IN SCOTLAND.-At Port
Nes-ock, in Wigtonshire, a large salt-water pond has been
formed for cod. It is a basin of 30 feet in depth and 160 feet
in circumference, hewn out from the solid rock, and com-
municating with the sea by one of those fissures which are
common to bold and precipitous coasts. Attached to it is a neat
gothic cottage for the accommodation of the fishermen, and the
rock is surmounted all round by a substantial stone wall 300
feet in circumference. From the inner or hack door of the lodge
a winding stairway conducts to the usual halting-place-a large
flat stone, projecting into the water, and commanding a view of
every part of the aquatic prison. When the tide is out the stone
is left completely dry, and here a stranger perceives with sur-
prise a hundred mouths simultaneously opened to greet his
arrival. The moment the fisherman crosses his threshold the
pond is agitated by the action of some hundred fins, and other-
wise thrown into a state of anarchy and confusion. Darting
from this, that, and the other corner, the whole population move
as it were to a common centre, elerate their snouts, lash their
tails, and jostle one another with such violence that on a first
view they actually seem to be menacing an attack on the poor
fisherman in place of the creel-full of

LONGEVITY. On the 25th of December last, Dennis
M'Kinley, of Sheans, near Ballycastle, departed this life, aged
177 years. He never had a day's sickness-could read the
smallest print without spectacles-usually rose at three o'clock
in the morning, and went to bed with the family. He died on
the same day of the month, and the same month on which he
was born. He was temperate in living.-Cork Constitution.
EFITS OF PASTION.-A man living at Treport, near Dieppe,
though 71 years of age, retained all the irregularities of his youth,
and consequently was on bad terms with his wife. A few days
ago he, in a fit of rage, first killed her with his gun, and then
attempted to destroy himself in the same manner; but not suc-
creding, he set fire to his bed, where he was look

REASON.
Reason, all bail! thou fairest boon of Heaven;
Thou sacred pledge to human nature given;
Prophetic star, that points a life to be ;
Existence in a desert, but for thee:
Science is thine-the vintage of the mind,
The light of life, that digniñes mankind ;
Science, the offspring of the soul's free scope.
Which lends to Truth the genuine fount of Hope.
Oh! may its rays dispel that chilling blast,
The powers of mind by ignorance o'ercast :
May thy true chart, thou guide to erring man,
Expand his soul the path of truth to scan;
Trace from its source the pure unsullied stream,
Free from the glimmerings of mysterious dreams;
Pellucid, pure, yea, as its source, divine,
Untinctured by the rancid ware of time;
Primeval streams upon its marble bel.
With no putrescence and contagion fed :
Bright and reflecting, tho' deep veiled its source,
Its current, Truth-Eternity its course.
Reason! 'tis thou uushrouds the mental skies,
And bids fair Freedom's sacred sun arise:
Without the light how listless would we be,
The mere brute grovelling in mortality!
When thou propels, 'tis Nature's mighty God,
Whose ray illumes and consecrates the road

THE CRIME OF POISONING.-The French chemists make
the following proposition, in order to render less frequent the
crime of poisoning, and to put on their guard those who may be
marked out as the victims of revenge, jealousy, or the like.
From 1824 to 1832 the number of individuals accused of
poisoning was 273, and it appeared that, in many instances the
intended victims had been saved by the bad taste communicated
to the food by the poisonous substance. It is, therefore, recom-
mended that it should be rendered compulsory to colour or
give a flavour of all poisonous substances which would not be
deteriorated by the admixture. For the latter purposes aloes
have been suggested, and of this many English as well as
French chemists have approved. It has also been recommended
to scent all poisons with the same odour-musk for instance.
NEW REMEDY AGAINST CANCER.-The Lancet has a notice
of a new remedy against cancer. It consists in the juice of a
tree which the Indians employ to envenom their arrows; and
it is also extensively employed by, the hordes which inhabit the
Barks of the Oronoko, as an anticarcinom
